[128:57]Somehow this is even more boring

[128:59]And we, you know, this has been in the news for a long time, but I haven't covered it

[129:03]because I really just hate talking about these fiscal matters to me. It's the most boring

[129:08]thing ever, but we're going to get into it because it is newsworthy. And in particular,

[129:13]the reason why we're talking about budget reconciliation, it matters for the economy.

[129:19]It matters for some other reasons. What really matters about this budget bill, the reason

[129:25]It's because whether or not we carry out the mass deportations has everything to do with

[129:31]this bill.

[129:32]Because if you've been following the mass deportation immigration story going back eight

[129:39]years, the problem has always been a lack of appropriations or that's what they say.

[129:46]There's no money for a border wall.

[129:47]They say they don't have the infrastructure.

[129:51]to deport a large number of people.

[129:54]They don't have enough ICE agents,

[129:56]they don't have enough Border Patrol agents,

[129:58]they don't have enough detention capacity

[130:00]to detain illegal immigrants while they adjudicate

[130:04]whether or not they should be deported.

[130:07]And so Trump is asking Congress for over $100 billion for border patrol and for ice.

[130:15]It's split between them.

[130:16]It's about 65 billion for border and it's actually a little bit more for deportations.

[130:21]It's about $81 billion for deportations.

[130:24]This all comes down to this budget reconciliation process.

[130:33]But, in order to get anything passed, anything substantial in the Senate, you need a supermajority.

[130:40]So, Republicans have 53 votes, technically 54 with the tiebreaker.

[130:45]You need 60 votes to get anything passed.

[130:48]And that has to do with some of the procedural rules.

[130:51]We're not gonna get into that.

[130:53]But basically, to get anything passed through the Senate

[130:55]that affects policy, that affects budget,

[130:57]that affects anything, you need 60 votes.

[131:00]That means Republicans are gonna need six Democrats.

[131:02]That's not gonna happen.

[131:04]Every year, there is a provision, and this is procedural.

[131:09]It's called the budget reconciliation process.

[131:13]Now, a bill that affects one of three things can pass the Senate with a simple majority.

[131:20]So you get this once per fiscal year, you can pass a bill with just 51 votes or a simple

[131:27]majority if it increases revenue, decreases spending or raises the debt ceiling.

[131:34]This has been in the procedural rulebook for 50 years.

[131:38]It's called the budget reconciliation process.

[131:40]And this is how the parties have gotten around the filibuster.

[131:44]This is how they get around that 60 vote threshold.

[131:47]And so once per year,

[131:48]technically they could do it three times.

[131:51]They could do a bill that increases revenue.

[131:53]They could do a bill that decreases spending.

[131:55]Or they could do a bill that increases the debt ceiling.

[131:58]Typically they combine it into one.

[132:00]And they could do each of those once per fiscal year.

[132:03]Again, usually they combine them into one bill.

[132:05]They have a bill that increases revenue, decreases spending,

[132:09]or increases the debt ceiling.

[132:12]And that way they can put in certain tax cuts,

[132:15]changes to the tax code.

[132:17]They could put in guidelines on how they're going

[132:20]to appropriate money.

[132:21]They could jam all this stuff in there,

[132:23]and they could pass it along party lines

[132:25]with a simple majority.

[132:27]That's what they're trying to do.

[132:28]That's what they've been trying to do.

[132:30]Ever since January, Republicans who have a very slim majority in the house, they have

[132:35]a two-vote majority there, they've got a three-vote majority in the Senate, which is

[132:39]a little more substantial, but not a super majority.

[132:42]They have been trying to cobble together their own majority to pass a budget reconciliation

[132:48]bill that does everything that Trump wants to do.

[132:58]The tax cuts in 2017 were passed using the same budget reconciliation process.

[133:04]And that is what cut the corporate tax rate from 35%, I think down to 24%.

[133:11]And it changed the marginal tax rates.

[133:14]It cut them a little bit.

[133:15]It doubled the standard deduction.

[133:17]It changed the tax code for a lot of people, but the big piece was that it significantly

[133:22]lowered the corporate tax rate.

[133:27]He wants to eliminate all taxes on tips.

[133:31]He wants to eliminate taxes on social security benefits.

[133:35]He wants to eliminate taxes on car loans.

[133:38]I guess interest on car loans or something like that.

[133:42]In addition, he wants to increase the military budget

[133:45]to a trillion dollars.

[133:47]And like I said, he wants money for.

[133:51]the border. He wants something like 140, 170, it was 170, now it's down to 140 billion,

[133:58]but he wants $140 billion for the border wall, for mass deportations, for other immigration

[134:05]enforcement.

[134:06]Now, here's the problem. So Trump wants all this stuff in one big, beautiful bill. That's

[134:12]been the debate. There are some in the Republican conference that want to pass multiple bills.

[134:18]They want to pass one bill with the tax cuts. They want to pass another bill with the spending

[134:22]increases. Trump says, no, I want everything in one agenda bill. I want one big, beautiful

[134:31]In order for Republicans to pass it, they have to use budget reconciliation because they don't have

[134:37]60 votes in the Senate. But to access the budget reconciliation process, the bill has to be

[134:45]budget neutral. It can't increase the deficit. So the tax cuts are going to increase the deficit.

[134:54]Increasing the budget for border security and military is going to increase the deficit.

[135:01]These are two things that increase spending, cutting taxes brings in less revenue and increasing

[135:07]spending spends more money.

[135:10]So

[135:11]How are they going to access budget reconciliation

[135:13]if they're increasing the deficit

[135:15]by cutting taxes and increasing spending?

[135:18]They have to offset these things

[135:20]by cutting spending elsewhere.

[135:22]How do you pay for the corporate tax rate cut?

[135:25]How do you pay for the tax cuts, which in the longterm are going to cost money in lost

[135:30]revenue?

[135:31]How are you going to pay for the increases in military and border spending?

[135:35]You have to cut spending elsewhere.

[135:37]That's the big disagreement.

[135:39]And that's a big part of why Elon Musk was deployed with Doge.

[135:43]They thought they were going to waltz in there and cut $2 trillion from the deficit.

[135:53]Then they said we're gonna cut one trillion. Then they said we're gonna cut less than that

[135:59]That was the goal

[136:01]It was a failure. They're barely they cut something like maybe 30 billion dollars. Maybe less. I mean, it's nothing

[136:07]They may not even when all is said and done have cut spending at all spending may go up actually

[136:14]But so this is the problem. They want to cut corporate tax rates. They want to cut tax

[136:19]on tips, cut tax on a lot of different things over time, social security, this and that.

[136:25]And they want 200 billion more, 150 billion more for military, 150 billion more for border.

[136:32]How do you pay for it? Well, they say they're going to cut that from Medicaid. That's healthcare

[136:38]for poor people, basically. They want to cut $880 billion for Medicaid. And then they

[136:47]want to get rid of all the subsidies in Biden's inflation reduction act.
